title: Promote and Pitch
developmental_frame: In the capstone, Communicate brings in the customers. At 11–12 a child can pull together persuasive writing (Q2) and live pitching (Q3) to promote a real launch and win over real potential customers — not just family. This is persuasion aimed at driving actual action (show up, buy), the highest-stakes communicating of the two years.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is getting real people interested enough to turn up or buy — persuasion that moves strangers to act. It's the difference between a good product nobody hears about and a launch people actually come to.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child promotes to real potential customers and pitches live at the launch, then sees whether their words brought people in. Persuasion that visibly works — a stranger showing up because of your promo, buying after your pitch — is thrilling and validating, and it makes every rehearsal worth it.

artifact: Promotional materials (a written promo plus a short spoken launch pitch) prepared, rehearsed, and delivered to real potential customers before and at the launch.
success_criteria: The written promo is honest, clear, and tempting to the target customer.
The spoken pitch has a hook and one clear message and was rehearsed.
Both were delivered to real potential customers (safely, with adult approval).

lessons.title: Write the promo
lessons.concept: Write a short promotional message using your persuasive-writing skills: a clear claim, a reason to care, aimed at your target customer. Keep it honest — over-promising wins one sale and loses all trust.
lessons.real_example: 'Spicy homemade snacks at Saturday's open day — RM2, made fresh, tested on the whole class. Come hungry!' Short, honest, tempting, and aimed right at snack-buyers.
lessons.apply_task: Write your promo message (a few lines) for your target customer. Make it honest and tempting.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Would your target customer stop and read this? Does it promise only what you can deliver?
lessons.feedback_task: Show your promo to a parent for approval and a 'would this make you come?' check.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend how to write an honest promo that still tempts people.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help polish the wording, but keep it true and in your voice — and never promise what your product can't deliver. Honesty is your reputation.
success_criteria: An honest, tempting promo aimed at the target customer is written.

lessons.title: Prepare the launch pitch
lessons.concept: Prepare a short spoken pitch for launch day using your Q3 pitching skills — a hook, one clear message, and a friendly close that invites the sale. This is what you say to someone standing at your stall or watching your drop.
lessons.real_example: 'Hungry? These are fresh spicy snacks I made and tested with my whole class — RM2 each, and the first batch always goes fast. Want to try one?' Hook, message, invite to buy.
lessons.apply_task: Write your 20–30 second launch pitch: hook, core message, and a friendly invitation to buy.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Does your pitch end by actually inviting the sale? Many pitches forget to ask.
lessons.feedback_task: Say your pitch to a parent and ask if it would make them want to try one.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone the shape of a good sales pitch: hook, message, invite.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can help draft it, but you deliver it live in your own voice — a pitch that sounds scripted or fake loses customers. Make it sound like you.
success_criteria: A short launch pitch with hook, message, and invitation is prepared.

lessons.title: Rehearse and refine
lessons.concept: Rehearse your pitch until it flows, and get a second pair of eyes on your promo. The launch is a one-shot moment — practising now means you'll be confident and clear when real customers are watching.
lessons.real_example: You practise the pitch ten times and it stops sounding stiff. A friend reads your promo and points out a confusing line. Both are far better before launch day than during it.
lessons.apply_task: Rehearse your pitch several times aloud, and refine your promo based on one piece of feedback.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where does your pitch still stumble? Those spots need one more run-through.
lessons.feedback_task: Do a full dress rehearsal for a family member and ask what would make it land better.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to someone why rehearsing a one-shot pitch matters so much.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Rehearsing your real voice is all you. AI can suggest a tweak to wording, but the confident delivery only comes from your own practice.
success_criteria: The pitch was rehearsed to fluency and the promo refined once.

lessons.title: Promote for real
lessons.concept: Put your promo out (safely, adult-approved) before the launch, and deliver your pitch to real customers on the day. This is the whole communication arc — writing and speaking to persuade — driving a real event.
lessons.real_example: Your promo goes up on the approved school board mid-week; on Saturday you pitch to everyone who stops by. Some came because of the promo; some buy because of the pitch. Persuasion, working.
lessons.apply_task: Share your promo (adult-approved) ahead of the launch, then deliver your pitch to real customers on launch day.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did your promo bring anyone in? Did your pitch turn lookers into buyers? What worked best?
lessons.feedback_task: Afterward, ask a customer what made them stop or buy — the promo, the pitch, or the product?
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone the story of promoting and pitching your launch and what landed.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Delivering to real customers is fully human and needs adult-approved, safe channels. This is your voice winning real people — no tool does it for you.
success_criteria: The promo was shared safely and the pitch delivered to real customers.
The child noted what drew customers in.
